common_name: Dukat Dill (High-Oil European Dill Variety with Superior Foliage Flavor)

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring several weeks before last frost; dill germinates in cool soil and does not transplant well (direct sow only). In zone 9, plant October–November for fall-winter production and February–March for a short spring crop before heat triggers bolting. Dukat has somewhat better slow-to-bolt characteristics than standard dill varieties, giving it slightly longer leaf production before it rushes to flower. However, all dill in zone 9 will bolt in summer heat — treat as a cool-season crop. For successive harvest, sow every 3–4 weeks. Dukat dill is valued for its exceptionally high essential oil content in the leaves (dill weed) compared to standard varieties, which translates to stronger, more aromatic flavor per unit of leaf material.

directions: Direct sow thinly in early spring or fall; thin to 6–9 inches. Harvest leaves before flowering for best flavor. Dukat Dill is a Danish dill variety (Dukat is a Danish word for a coin or ducat) developed and selected for high essential oil content in the leaves — the characteristic that translates to superior flavor intensity in dill weed, the fresh or dried feathery leaf material used in cooking rather than the seeds. All dill contains aromatic volatile oils (primarily carvone and limonene), but the proportion and intensity of these oils varies substantially among varieties, with Dukat consistently showing higher leaf oil content than standard American dill varieties in comparative trials. This difference is directly perceptible in the kitchen: Dukat dill weed has a stronger, more clearly identifiable dill aroma and flavor than standard varieties harvested at the same stage, making it preferable for recipes where dill flavor should be distinct without using large quantities of herb. The plants are tall (3–4 feet) with the characteristic ferny, blue-green dill foliage and produce yellow flower umbels at maturity. Dukat has also been noted for slightly more vigorous early leaf growth than standard varieties, giving it a head start on production. Like all dill, it is a favorite host plant for black swallowtail butterfly caterpillars, which feed on dill, fennel, parsley, and related Apiaceae members — planting dill in zone 9 gardens supports local butterfly populations while providing a culinary herb.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Dukat dill variety notes and essential oil content comparison
The Cook's Garden — Dukat dill superior flavor documentation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Dill Production in Florida and the Gulf South

common_name: Golden Lemon Thyme (Gold-Leafed Ornamental Lemon-Scented Thyme, Aureus Thyme)

planting.note: Plant divisions or cuttings in spring or fall; Golden Lemon Thyme does not come true from seed and is propagated vegetatively. In zone 9, thyme plants are often treated as short-lived perennials — they survive winters well but can decline in the intense summer heat and humidity of the Gulf Coast; provide excellent drainage and avoid overhead irrigation to minimize root rot and fungal issues. Plant in a raised bed or sloped location with gritty, well-drained soil for best longevity. Golden Lemon Thyme's gold-and-green variegated foliage is particularly valuable in zone 9 gardens during the cooler months (October–April) when the colors are most vivid; summer heat may cause some color fading. The lemon scent is distinct from the camphor-pine scent of standard cooking thyme and has culinary value in sweet and savory applications.

directions: Plant cuttings or divisions in well-drained gritty soil; harvest lightly without removing more than a third of the plant. Golden Lemon Thyme (Thymus × citriodorus 'Aureus') is a hybrid thyme cultivar — a cross between common thyme (Thymus vulgaris) and a lemon-scented thyme species — selected for its variegated gold-and-green foliage pattern, which gives it significant ornamental value beyond the culinary use shared with all thyme varieties. The 'Aureus' (meaning golden in Latin) name describes the characteristic yellow-gold coloration of the leaf margins and tips, which contrasts with the green center of each small oval leaf and creates a bright, luminous appearance in garden plantings. This golden variegation is consistent throughout the growing season, making Golden Lemon Thyme one of the most visually distinctive ground cover herbs available for sunny, dry garden situations. The fragrance is the combination of thyme's characteristic aromatics with a distinct lemon overlay — lighter and more citrus-like than standard cooking thyme, which has a more herbaceous, almost medicinal scent. This lemon-thyme fragrance makes Golden Lemon Thyme particularly useful in cooking applications where the pungency of standard thyme would be too dominant: it pairs well with fish, chicken, vegetables, and in preparations where a lighter herbal-citrus note is wanted. The plants bloom in late spring with clusters of tiny lavender to pale pink flowers that attract bees and other pollinators — the combination of gold-green foliage and lavender flowers at bloom time is particularly attractive in rock gardens and herb beds. In zone 9, Golden Lemon Thyme performs well in cool months and requires excellent drainage year-round to survive summer heat and humidity.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Thymus × citriodorus 'Aureus' ornamental thyme description
Logee's Tropical Plants — Golden lemon thyme variety cultivation notes
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Lemon thyme variety comparison and culinary use

common_name: Blue Spice Basil (Compact Mild Anise-Flavored Basil with Lavender Flower Bracts)

planting.note: Start indoors 3–4 weeks before transplanting or direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; Blue Spice Basil is compact (12–16 inches) and well-suited to container growing. In zone 9, plant March–May for spring-summer production and August–September for fall production. Blue Spice has better heat tolerance than standard Genovese basil, making it a more practical choice for Gulf Coast zone 9 where midsummer heat stresses large-leafed Italian types. The name 'Blue Spice' refers to the distinctly lavender-blue flower bracts that develop as the plant matures — technically the plant is flowering at this stage, which reduces leaf production, but the blue flower clusters are ornamental and edible, with the same mild anise-sweet flavor as the leaves. Pinch out flower buds frequently to extend leaf production.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 70–80°F or direct sow after last frost. Pinch growing tips frequently to encourage bushy growth. Harvest leaves before flowers fully open. Blue Spice Basil is a compact sweet basil variety selected for its distinctive lavender-blue flower bracts and mild anise-sweet flavor profile, which is lighter and less intensely clove-like than standard Genovese basil. The variety produces small, bright green oval leaves on compact bushy plants with a tidy, mounding habit that makes it well-suited to container and edging use, as well as herb gardens where a more compact, ornamental basil is preferred over the large-leafed Italian varieties. The 'Blue Spice' name reflects the plant's most distinctive feature: the flower spikes develop conspicuously blue-lavender bracts (the small leaves associated with each flower) that give the blooming plant a distinctly blue-tinted appearance unusual in the basil family. This blue coloration makes Blue Spice one of the most ornamental of the sweet basil group, bridging the gap between standard culinary basil and the more intensely colored Thai basils. The flavor is in the mild, sweet end of the basil spectrum — less intense than Genovese, with a gentle anise note and sweet basil fragrance — making it appropriate for fresh use in salads, as a garnish, and in preparations where a subtle basil flavor is preferred over the intensity of full-strength Italian types. The flowers and bracts are edible with the same flavor as the leaves and make an attractive edible garnish.
sources: Burpee Seeds — Blue Spice basil variety description and growing notes
Park Seed — Blue Spice basil compact ornamental-culinary variety notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Basil Production in the Gulf South

common_name: Christmas Basil (Compact Heat-Tolerant Clove-Scented Hybrid Basil for Extended Season)

planting.note: Start indoors 3–4 weeks before transplanting or direct sow after last frost in warm soil; Christmas Basil is a hybrid between sweet basil (Ocimum basilicum) and Thai/Asian basil types, combining the leaf size and general appearance of sweet basil with the more intense, clove-like fragrance of Thai basil and improved heat tolerance. In zone 9, plant March–May and August–September. Christmas Basil is significantly more heat and disease tolerant than standard Genovese basil — it is better able to withstand the Gulf Coast's summer heat without declining and shows good resistance to Fusarium wilt and downy mildew, the two most damaging pathogens affecting basil production in warm-humid climates. The name 'Christmas' refers not to the holiday but to the compact, rounded, mounded growth habit that some gardeners liken to a Christmas tree shape.

directions: Start seeds indoors or direct sow after last frost in warm soil. Pinch flower buds to extend leaf production. Harvest continuously. Christmas Basil is a hybrid basil variety that represents a key development in warm-climate basil breeding: the combining of sweet basil's mild, widely-appealing flavor with the improved heat tolerance and disease resistance of Asian basil species, particularly Thai basil (Ocimum × citriodorum). Standard Genovese-type sweet basil, while ideal for Italian cooking and widely preferred for pesto, has two significant limitations in zone 9 and other hot-humid climates: it is susceptible to Fusarium wilt (a soilborne fungal disease that wilts and kills plants) and downy mildew (an airborne pathogen that devastates basil foliage in warm-humid conditions), and it performs poorly in sustained heat, often bolting and declining during the Gulf Coast summer. Christmas Basil's hybrid origin provides significantly better tolerance to both heat and these pathogens — plants remain productive and healthy through conditions that would collapse standard Genovese types. The flavor of Christmas Basil occupies an intermediate position between sweet basil and Thai basil: more complex and spicy than plain Genovese, with distinct clove and anise notes from the Thai parentage, but milder than full-strength Thai basil. This flavor profile is versatile — appropriate for Italian applications (especially where some aromatic complexity is desired), for Southeast Asian-inspired dishes, and as a general culinary herb where the assertive clove character of straight Thai basil would be too dominant. The compact, mounding growth habit (12–20 inches) keeps plants tidy and manageable in containers and herb beds.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Christmas basil variety notes and heat-tolerance documentation
Harris Seeds — Christmas basil hybrid performance and disease resistance notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Basil Fusarium Wilt and Downy Mildew Management

common_name: Osmin Basil (Compact Dark Purple Basil, Ornamental and Culinary Dwarf Purple Variety)

planting.note: Start indoors 3–4 weeks before transplanting or direct sow after last frost; Osmin is a compact, uniformly purple basil that stays significantly smaller (12–14 inches) than Dark Opal or other large-leafed purple basil types, making it better suited to containers, borders, and formal herb gardens where a tidy, predictable size is wanted. In zone 9, plant March–May and August–September. The deep purple coloration of Osmin is consistent and stable throughout the plant's life — unlike some purple basil varieties that may revert to green in high heat, Osmin maintains good color through warm summer conditions. Pinch out flower buds to extend the productive leaf stage; the pink to lavender flowers of Osmin are also ornamental and edible.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 70–80°F or direct sow after last frost. Pinch growing tips frequently to maintain compact form. Harvest leaves as needed. Osmin Purple Basil is a compact, dark-purple ornamental-culinary basil variety selected specifically for consistent, uniform deep-purple coloration and a dwarf, tidy growth habit smaller than the standard Dark Opal and other large purple basil varieties. The name Osmin may reference Ottoman or Turkish naming conventions, reflecting the Central Asian and Middle Eastern origins of purple basil cultivar development. Purple basil varieties derive their color from anthocyanin pigments — the same class of compounds responsible for purple coloration in red cabbage, blueberries, and eggplant skin — and have been cultivated for centuries both for their visual distinction and for the herb's culinary uses. In the ornamental garden context, Osmin's uniformly purple foliage and compact size make it one of the most useful purple basil varieties for edging, formal herb garden designs, and container arrangements, where the consistent dark color provides a reliable foil for green herbs and bright flowers. The culinary value of purple basil is in the distinctive peppery, slightly clove-flavored fragrance (same family as green sweet basil but somewhat less intensely sweet) combined with the visual effect of purple-tinted vinegars (one of the most popular uses of purple basil), herbed oils, and fresh presentations where the purple leaf color adds visual interest. Osmin's smaller leaf size compared to Dark Opal means individual leaves are more delicate in presentations and salads. The flavor and fragrance are typical of the purple basil group: spicy, mildly clove-like, and sweet with less of the intense Italian character of Genovese.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Osmin purple basil compact variety description
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Osmin basil dark purple ornamental variety notes
Richters Herbs — Purple basil variety comparison and Osmin description
